topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips. I stared at the meticulously typed paragraphs, the accolades, the carefully curated anecdotes that painted a picture of a life I barely recognized. Every sentence was a calculated move, a stroke of prose designed to flatter, to diminish, to control. A tightness coiled in my chest, a hard knot that made every breath shallow. I traced a line of text describing my ‘defining triumph,’ and a sharp, involuntary shudder ran through me. Who had held this pen? Who had decided the shape of my existence? The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I crumpled the page slowly, the sound a small, defeated sigh in the quiet office.

[story 2]

"It's all so… polished," I murmured, the words tasting like ash. The biography lay spread across the mahogany desk, a monument to someone else's interpretation of me. I picked up the pen they must have used—a heavy, expensive instrument—and turned it over in my hand, feeling the cold metal against my palm. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I wanted to rip the whole thing to shreds, to scatter the ink across the floor, but my hands were strangely steady, betraying a deep, simmering fury. I stood up, pacing the length of the room, my boots clicking sharply on the marble. Every glance toward the door felt like an accusation.

[story 3]

I reread the section detailing my early struggles, the descriptions so vivid, so painfully accurate in a way that felt invasive. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. This narrative, this carefully constructed portrait of my journey, was a theft. It wasn't just inaccurate; it was a complete erasure of my own messy truth. I slammed the binder shut, the sound echoing unnervingly loud in the cavernous library. I walked to the window, pulling back the heavy velvet curtain just enough to let in a sliver of indifferent daylight. My shoulders slumped, heavy with a profound sense of being misrepresented, and I didn't move for a long time.
